features.name: North Branch of the Chicago River (pre-channelization course, Belmont Ave to Lawrence Ave)
features.note: Approximate reconstruction of the North Branch's natural, meandering course before the Sanitary District's Belmont-to-Lawrence channel-straightening project (per doc 08's own 1921 Engineering Data citation: work dated May 10, 1904 - Dec 2, 1907, a 2.2-mile reach). No historic survey plat of the pre-1904 meanders was located in the archive; this line is a plausible sinuous path threaded through the same 2.2-mile envelope as the modern straightened channel (itself pulled from OpenStreetMap), anchored to the same endpoints at Lawrence Ave (~41.9687,-87.7011, at today's North Branch Pumping Station) and Belmont Ave (~41.9396,-87.6927). The amplitude and wavelength of the meanders are illustrative, not surveyed.

features.name: South Branch of the Chicago River (pre-1930 course, West Loop bend near the old Main Post Office)
features.note: Approximate historic alignment of the South Branch through the West Loop before the Sanitary District/City's 1928-1930 South Branch relocation project moved the channel roughly 500 ft west (the straightened channel is the one that runs today past Union Station and under the Old Main Post Office at 433 W Van Buren St, ~41.8760,-87.6388). This project is referenced in this archive's docs but not tabulated in detail; no historic survey plat was located, so the eastward bend shown here is a plausible envelope (anchored to the modern channel at both ends, peaking near the Post Office site) rather than a surveyed centerline. Flagged for follow-up against a period Sanborn map or Sanitary District plat if one becomes available.

features.name: Lake Calumet (approximate outline, c. 1890)
features.note: Approximate pre-industrial outline, roughly twice today's ~442-acre open-water area and extending well south of the modern shoreline (today's lake spans ~41.6728-41.6877N; this outline runs ~41.646-41.696N, i.e. roughly 103rd St to 130th St, per Colten's description in doc 15 of a marsh 'about 3 mi long and 1.5 mi wide' surveyed in 1834). Doc 15 explicitly flags that Colten's 3x1.5-mile figure cannot be reconciled with the modern 442-acre figure or the USGS's 'about half filled' estimate — this polygon should be read as an illustrative envelope of the lake-plus-marsh floodplain, not a surveyed shoreline. No period plat or Corps survey map was digitized in this archive to trace an exact 1890 shoreline.

features.name: Lake Calumet (approximate intermediate outline, c. 1950)
features.note: Illustrative intermediate outline between the ~1890 marsh extent and today's ~442-acre harbor, reflecting the documented 1940 municipal-landfill dike at 110th St that filled the lake's entire northern quarter (doc 15) and decades of slag/dredge-spoil filling around the perimeter, but predating the final decades of filling that left 'about half' the historic lake filled by the early 1990s (USGS, cited in doc 15). Boundary is an interpolated envelope, not a digitized period map; no 1950-specific USGS quadrangle or Corps survey was traced in this archive.

features.name: Hyde Lake (approximate outline)
features.note: Small lake formed 1872-1881 when falling Wolf Lake/Berry Lake water levels exposed a separating sand ridge, 'almost completely filled by 1902' (USGS, doc 15) -- doc 15 explicitly could not resolve Hyde Lake's exact footprint: the 1892 USGS Calumet quad and an 1894 Corps map place it immediately southwest of Wolf Lake and northeast of the Calumet River's turning basins (inside today's industrial/CID Landfill corridor, roughly the coordinates used here), while a commonly cited proxy location near 122nd-130th & Torrence/Brainard (today's restored Hegewisch Marsh Park, ~41.6554,-87.5657, about half a mile southwest of this polygon) may describe overlapping but not identical ground. This polygon follows the primary-source placement; the alternate is noted here per doc 15's own Open Questions. Deviated from the task brief's suggested coordinates for this reason -- see note.

features.name: Wolf Lake (single open lake, before the late-1950s Skyway-fill causeways)
features.note: Approximate outline of Wolf Lake as a single, undiked, cross-border (IL-IN) open lake before the Illinois portion was diked into 5 impoundments and the Indiana portion into 3 (drained/dredged section by section for Chicago Skyway fill, Skyway opened 1958 -- doc 15). Total lake area today (804 ac: 419 IL + 385 IN, per doc 15) is not dramatically different from the historic figure since most of the lake's perimeter was only lightly filled (the main exception being several hundred acres of northwest-shore wetland filled with slag for a 1950s Nike missile site, now Eggers Woods -- included as open water here). Outline is a smoothed union of the modern impoundments' combined footprint with internal dikes removed; no pre-1950s survey plat was located in this archive.

features.name: Cal-Sag Channel (original 1922 narrow channel, width-change only)
features.note: Same centerline alignment as the modern Cal-Sag Channel (from OpenStreetMap) -- the 1911-1922 channel followed essentially the same 16.2-mile Blue Island-to-Sag-Junction route it does today (doc 02/15). What changed is cross-section, not alignment: originally 60 ft wide through rock (36 ft bottom/116 ft waterline in earth), widened to 225 ft (9 ft navigation depth) during the 1955-1971 USACE Calumet-Sag Modernization (doc 02, doc 15). Geometry here is a width-change-only flag, not a distinct historic centerline.

features.name: Mud Lake / Chicago Portage (approximate vestige corridor)
features.note: Optional, low-confidence sketch of the historic Mud Lake / Chicago Portage corridor -- the shallow seasonal wetland that connected the Des Plaines River (near today's Chicago Portage National Historic Site, Harlem Ave & 47th St, Lyons/Riverside, 41.8049,-87.8054) to the South Branch of the Chicago River's west-fork headwaters, used by Marquette and Jolliet in 1673 and progressively drained/ditched (Ogden-Wentworth Ditch, 1871 deep cut) through the 19th century. No source in this archive (docs 01-16) maps Mud Lake's boundaries directly; this line is a rough corridor sketch between the two well-documented endpoints, not a traced shoreline, and is included only because the task brief flagged it as optional. Treat with low confidence.
